issueModules.title: No valid certificate of occupancy bars rent (MDL 301/302)
issueModules.when: Building/unit occupied contrary to its certificate of occupancy (illegal combination/subdivision of units, commercial space converted to apartments). Open DOB/ECB/OATH violations stating 'occupancy contrary to c/o' are prima facie evidence the violation exists. Bar on rent applies building-wide, not just the altered unit; Chazon is strict — no equitable exception, no requirement the violation affect the specific unit or structural integrity
issueModules.strength: STRONG — Court of Appeals (Chazon) + multiple App Term & Civ Ct decisions; appellate support (GVS Vargas, 49 Bleecker, Pei-De Tsai)

issueModules.title: Notice of Petition fails to use mandatory court form
issueModules.when: NoP does not conform to 22 NYCRR 208.42(b) / AO 163/19

issueModules.title: Overcharge discovery for post-HSTPA tenancies (Harvey)
issueModules.when: Tenancy began after 6/14/2019 and the rent history looks unreliable. Harvey (App. Div., 1st Dept) holds that where the first lease post-dates HSTPA and no pre-HSTPA overcharge is alleged, there is no retroactivity problem; the tenant need NOT assert fraud — an unreliable rent history (e.g., an unsupported IAI increase, double-counted items) raises a fact question warranting CPLR 408 discovery. Creston (Bronx) applies Harvey: a certified DHCR history showing an unexplained increase plus DOB records showing no permitted work = ample need for discovery; tenant need only show the rent history is unreliable
issueModules.strength: STRONG — App. Div. 1st Dept (Harvey) + Bronx Civ Ct (Creston)

issueModules.title: Rent-impairing violations -> up to 100% abatement / no rent recoverable (MDL 302-a)
issueModules.when: Open HPD rent-impairing violation (order number marked with an asterisk; e.g., order 507/567 roof leak, rodent infestation) uncorrected more than six months after DHPD mailed notice to the owner. A common-area / owner-controlled-area violation (roof, courtyard, cellar) is deemed to exist in each resident's unit, and the landlord's claim that the tenant denied access to the apartment will NOT rebut the defense where the condition is in a common area
issueModules.strength: trial-level, multiple aligned decisions; pairs with habitability

issueModules.title: Farkas six-factor ample-need discovery test (CPLR 408)
issueModules.when: Building a discovery motion in a summary proceeding. Movant need not show every Farkas factor (Mautner-Glick); demonstrated need + no prejudice + narrowly tailored demands suffices. For overcharge, post-Harvey the burden is reduced — showing the rent history is unreliable is enough (Creston). Landlords are likewise entitled to succession discovery over a relevant period, including facts bearing on the bona fides of the claim (Singh)

issueModules.title: GCEL non-renewal holdover grounded on nonpayment requires a notice stating a good-faith amount owed, the months owed, and the increase percentage
issueModules.when: Good Cause non-renewal holdover where the 'good cause' is nonpayment, and the 90-day termination notice omits the rent amount, the periods owed, or the percentage of any increase — defect precludes the tenant's pre-warrant cure right and requires dismissal
issueModules.strength: trial-level; multiple Civ. Ct. decisions aligning (verify currency)

issueModules.title: Domestic-violence / mental-health incapacity can supply a reasonable excuse to vacate a default under CPLR 5015(a)(1)
issueModules.when: Respondent defaulted while incapacitated by trauma/DV (hospitalization, PTSD, depression with medical documentation), now has counsel and a potentially meritorious defense

issueModules.title: GCEL (RPL 216) creates no independent cause of action; early-termination holdovers still require a conditional limitation, not a mere condition subsequent
issueModules.when: Holdover premised on a lease option-to-terminate clause (condition subsequent) rather than an automatic conditional limitation; or landlord argues Good Cause itself authorizes the holdover. A bare option-to-terminate gives only ejectment under RPAPL art. 6, not a summary proceeding
issueModules.strength: trial-level (applies 3d Dept law); persuasive in NYC — verify against 1st/2d Dept

issueModules.title: Fraud exception to the overcharge lookback needs only 'sufficient indicia of fraud' / a 'colorable claim' — not every element of common-law fraud; post-2023 amendments restore the Thornton/Grimm totality test
issueModules.when: Overcharge claim where landlord invokes the four-year (or RSL 26-516(a)) lookback. The 2023/2024 RSL/RSC amendments (L 2023 ch 760; L 2024 ch 95) apply to all claims pending as of 12/22/2023, direct courts back to Thornton and Grimm, permit records of any age, and use a 'totality of the circumstances' test for whether the owner 'knowingly engaged' in a fraudulent deregulation scheme. Alekna and Cox/Gomes confirm no impermissible retroactivity in applying the totality test to pending cases; managing agents who participate in fraud are liable
issueModules.strength: STRONG — Court of Appeals (Aras/Burrows; Thornton; Grimm; Liggett) + App. Div. 1st Dept (Alekna, Cox) and 2d Dept (Gomes); App Term (Goldstein)

issueModules.title: Where a deceased tenant of record's unexpired lease was in effect at commencement, the estate is a necessary party and the failure to name it warrants dismissal
issueModules.when: Holdover (including licensee) against an occupant after the tenant of record died, where an unexpired lease was still in effect at commencement but the estate / a Surrogate's-appointed representative was not named. Note: court may order joinder (CPLR 1003) rather than dismiss — fact-dependent
issueModules.strength: trial-level; turns on whether the lease was genuinely unexpired

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): a missing estate may NOT be a necessary party where the CPLR 1001(b) factors favor proceeding without joinder
issueModules.when: You move to dismiss for non-joinder of an estate, but the absent party's interests are not immediately/inequitably affected (e.g., no immediate sale of the home, and the movant could just as easily seek appointment of a representative)
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish by showing the absent party IS immediately/inequitably affected and an unexpired lease exists (cf. Calvary/Hicks)

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): a belief that the papers were 'fake,' without diligent inquiry, is not a reasonable excuse to vacate a default
issueModules.when: Respondent seeks to vacate a default claiming they thought the summons was illegitimate, but had received prior demand letters / court correspondence and made no real inquiry. (Frederick was a civil rent-debt action, but the 5015 reasoning applies to summary proceedings too)
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish by showing diligent inquiry, no prior notice, or pairing with a strong meritorious defense

issueModules.title: CARES Act requires a 30-day notice (not a 14-day rent demand) for any building with a federally backed mortgage; once the mortgage is purchased or securitized by Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac, private origination is irrelevant
issueModules.when: Nonpayment proceeding where the building has (or had) a federally backed multifamily mortgage loan. Pull the mortgage/assignment history; if it was assigned to Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac, the property is a 'covered dwelling' and a 14-day rent demand is defective. A defective predicate notice cannot be amended and warrants dismissal.
issueModules.strength: trial-level (Bronx); directly on point and tenant-favorable

issueModules.title: Rent-impairing violations open over six months entitle the tenant to a 100% rent abatement under MDL § 302-a; the landlord's lack-of-access rebuttal fails where the violations are not inside the tenant's apartment
issueModules.when: Nonpayment proceeding where HPD has issued rent-impairing violations (e.g., public hallway, roof) open more than six months. Deposit rent into escrow and move to dismiss. Landlord's 'no access' argument under § 302-a(3)(b) only defeats the defense for violations located inside the unit. Landlord's failure to file a certification of compliance is prima facie proof the violation is uncorrected (Admin Code § 27-2115(f)(7)).
issueModules.strength: App Term (2d Dept); strong tenant defense

issueModules.title: Failure to plead a tenant's Section 8 status (and service of the predicate notice on the PHA), and failure to plead the apartment's true regulatory status, are RPAPL § 741 defects; cumulative, non-obvious, unexplained errors are not curable by amendment and require dismissal
issueModules.when: Nonpayment or holdover where the petition omits Section 8/subsidy status, or misstates regulatory status (e.g., pleads 'fewer than six units / unregulated' while concealing a 421-a or J-51 tax benefit that makes the unit rent-stabilized). The Section 8 pleading defect has 'repeatedly been found prejudicial' and not amendable; regulatory-status errors that the court and a pro se tenant could not readily detect likewise resist amendment. RPAPL § 741 protects the regulatory system and tenancies generally, not just the tenant at bar.

issueModules.title: In a failure-to-renew holdover, the landlord bears the burden to prove the renewal offer was based on a lawful rent; a post-Roberts/Gersten J-51 landlord that treated the unit as deregulated must offer a proper initial rent-stabilized lease, and a renewal stating a legal regulated rent the tenant never paid, with no rider explaining the calculation, is defective
issueModules.when: Holdover for failure to renew a rent-stabilized lease where the building received J-51 benefits and was wrongly treated as deregulated (Roberts/Gersten). If the renewal offer (a) recites a 'legal regulated rent' never established in any lease the tenant signed, (b) lacks a rider explaining how the rent was calculated, and (c) is served as a 'renewal' when no initial stabilized lease ever existed, the tenant is justified in refusing to sign and the petition fails to state a cause of action.

issueModules.title: A petitioner's failure to use the mandatory Notice of Petition form (AO 163/19; 22 NYCRR § 208.42[b]) is a substantive defect akin to a defective predicate notice; leave to add the defense is freely granted and the landlord may NOT cure by amending the notice of petition
issueModules.when: Any summary proceeding commenced after the mandatory form took effect (in force 5+ years) using a non-conforming notice of petition. Move to amend the answer to add the defense (granted absent real prejudice — late amendment alone is not prejudice). The landlord's cross-motion to amend the notice of petition into compliance should be denied, since a substantive NOP error is treated like an unamendable predicate-notice defect.
issueModules.strength: trial-level (NY County); collects supporting trial-level authority incl. Bronx

issueModules.title: A loft landlord out of compliance with the Loft Law (no residential C of O and no extension) may neither recover rent nor maintain any proceeding for possession for nonpayment of rent (MDL §§ 302[1][b], 285[1])
issueModules.when: Nonpayment or ejectment for a loft/interim-multiple-dwelling unit where the owner has not obtained a residential certificate of occupancy and is not in Loft Law compliance. The statutory bar is absolute; relabeling the case as ejectment does not avoid it.

issueModules.title: Reasonable reliance by the tenant is NOT required for the fraud exception to the four-year lookback rule; the tenant need only put forth 'sufficient indicia of fraud' or a 'colorable claim' of a fraudulent scheme, not every element of common-law fraud
issueModules.when: Rent overcharge claim where the landlord asserts a four-year (lookback) statute-of-limitations bar. To open the rent history for the limited purpose of ascertaining whether fraud occurred, plead colorable indicia of a fraudulent scheme (the exception does not itself recalculate base-date rent or revive time-barred recovery).

issueModules.title: Registering an arbitrary, fictitious first rent that was never actually charged and paid, in order to take excessive increases, constitutes fraud against the rent-regulatory system; a preferential-rent stratagem in contravention of RSC § 2521.1(g) can supply the indicia of fraud
issueModules.when: Overcharge claim on remittitur/CPLR 3211 posture where the registration history shows an inflated or never-collected 'first rent,' or use of preferential rents to inflate the legal regulated rent. Construe the complaint in the light most favorable to the tenant; a bare 'no overcharge' assertion does not defeat a sufficiently pleaded fraud claim.
issueModules.strength: App Div (1st Dept) on remittitur from the Court of Appeals

issueModules.title: A landlord that offered a market-rate lease post-Roberts while receiving J-51 benefits cannot claim good-faith reliance on prior DHCR guidance; assumptions about regulatory status may amount to willful ignorance — but Regina Metro still bars retroactive HSTPA application to pre-HSTPA overcharges
issueModules.when: Overcharge claim involving J-51 deregulation after Roberts. Useful to defeat the landlord's reliance/good-faith narrative, but note the limits: on a contested record, summary judgment on fraud 'as a matter of law' may be denied for scienter questions, and pre-HSTPA overcharges are governed by pre-HSTPA law (four-year lookback; Regina Metro).
issueModules.strength: App Div (1st Dept); mixed — favorable on willful ignorance, cautionary on Regina/treble
issueModules.posture: NEUTRAL — mixed; cite for the willful-ignorance point, mind the Regina limits

issueModules.title: Suing a tenant for substantially more than owed while the building is unregistered can support GBL § 349, gross negligence, and (against the landlord's attorneys) Judiciary Law § 487 claims; an unregistered multiple dwelling cannot collect rent
issueModules.when: Affirmative/offensive posture after defending a frivolous or inflated nonpayment: the building was unregistered (barring rent collection under the MDL), the demand overstated the amount owed, and counsel kept litigating with knowledge of the misrepresentations. Supports a later plenary action for GBL § 349 (consumer-oriented deception), gross negligence, and Judiciary Law § 487 against the attorneys.

issueModules.title: In an HP proceeding, a respondent (including HPD as owner) that defies an Order to Correct may be held in civil contempt; the court can credit rent paid while the unit was uninhabitable and bar further rent until conditions (e.g., electricity) are corrected; offering an alternative unit that is itself uninhabitable does not satisfy the order
issueModules.when: Tenant-initiated HP proceeding to compel repairs where the owner ignores an Order to Correct. Move for contempt; seek a rent credit/abatement and an order that no rent accrue until the unit is habitable. Electricity and other essential services fall within the warranty of habitability (Park West v Mitchell).

issueModules.title: CPLR 308(2) service at a former address — where the defendant no longer resides and the landlord knew the current address — is invalid, and the default is vacated without a traverse hearing; a notice of appearance neither waives nor confers personal jurisdiction (CPLR 320[b], 3211[e] are exclusive)
issueModules.when: Default judgment (often a plenary rent-debt action, but the reasoning applies generally) where the affidavit of service shows delivery/mailing to a unit the tenant had already vacated. Moving to dismiss under CPLR 3211(a)(8) preserves the objection even after a prior motion or a bare notice of appearance. Note also: this court flagged AI-fabricated case citations in opposing counsel's papers and nearly disregarded the entire submission.

issueModules.title: A party on notice of possible litigation must preserve relevant evidence (e.g., video footage); negligent destruction warrants sanctions, with an adverse-inference charge as the proportionate remedy and preclusion/striking reserved for more egregious conduct
issueModules.when: Contextual litigation tool — e.g., where a landlord or managing agent destroys repair records, inspection video, or surveillance footage relevant to a habitability/harassment dispute after being on notice of a claim. Seek an adverse inference; preclusion may be available where the spoliator made the destroyed evidence the crux of its own defense.
issueModules.strength: App Div (2d Dept); general civil doctrine, niche in housing

issueModules.title: Proof of proper mailing (e.g., a properly executed affidavit of service) creates a rebuttable presumption of receipt five days after mailing (CPLR 2103[b][2]); a bare denial of receipt does not rebut it — a sworn, nonconclusory denial is required
issueModules.when: Procedural reference where receipt/timeliness of a mailed notice is disputed. Cuts both ways: to challenge a landlord's claimed service, prepare a specific sworn denial (not a bald 'I never got it'); to anticipate the landlord's reliance on a mailing affidavit.
issueModules.strength: App Div (2d Dept); neutral procedural rule

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): habitability/conditions already litigated to judgment as a defense in a nonpayment proceeding are barred by res judicata and collateral estoppel from being relitigated in a later plenary harassment/breach action; building-condition harassment under Admin Code § 27-2004(a)(48)(ii)(b) also requires a predicate violation
issueModules.when: CAUTION when conditions have been (or will be) litigated in a nonpayment proceeding and the tenant later wants a separate plenary action on the same facts. New York's transactional res judicata bars the later claims even if framed differently. Practice point: raise all conditions-based claims — including affirmative harassment counterclaims — within the housing proceeding, and ensure any predicate HPD violations post-date and are attributable to the current owner.
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish; use as a claim-preservation warning

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): breach of the warranty of habitability is not a defense available to an occupant who is not a party to the lease; restoration to possession may be conditioned on payment of arrears
issueModules.when: CAUTION where the person seeking to defend or be restored is a non-party occupant rather than the lease tenant. Establish the client's tenancy/lease status (or succession rights) before relying on a habitability defense; an occupant's repair-based withholding argument will fail on standing.
issueModules.strength: App Term (2d Dept); landlord-favorable on occupant standing

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): to win a preliminary injunction on a warranty-of-habitability theory the tenant must show likelihood of success (extensiveness of breach, health/safety effect, and measures the landlord took); a notice to cure is sufficient if it gives a good-faith accounting, the default period, cure instructions, and the consequences of noncompliance
issueModules.when: CAUTION when seeking a PI to compel repairs or attacking a notice to cure. Largely-corrected violations plus the tenant's own denial of access will defeat the likelihood-of-success showing; a facially complete notice to cure (accounting + period + instructions + consequences) will be upheld. Document access provided and the persistence/severity of conditions to overcome this.

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (agency-favorable): the four-month Article 78 statute of limitations to challenge a Section 8 subsidy termination runs from the tenant's receipt of the termination (T3) letter / actual notice; later requests for restoration are mere continued negotiation and do not toll it
issueModules.when: CAUTION when advising on a challenge to a Section 8/subsidy termination. Calendar the four-month CPLR 217(1) deadline from the termination notice, not from a later restoration denial; do not assume renewed administrative requests reset the clock.

issueModules.title: Low relevance: a small-claims plaintiff cannot recover for property of a deceased nonparty tenant absent privity, contractual relationship, or status as the estate's representative
issueModules.when: Rarely applicable. Contextual only — standing/privity limits on small-claims property recovery against a landlord.

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): when an MDL 301/302 c/o defense FAILS
issueModules.when: The c/o defense can fail where (a) the building has a valid I-card that serves the function of a c/o and the change (e.g., number of bedrooms) does not require a new c/o, with no actual outstanding violation — only the tenant's supposition (Agresti); (b) the c/o issue was already litigated, so law of the case / collateral estoppel bars relitigation (Agresti); or (c) the tenant stopped paying all rent and a DHCR order already fixed the legal rent with arrears exceeding any overcharge (Cornine — ejectment in Sup Ct). Agresti and Cornine were not summary nonpayment proceedings
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — confirm an actual open c/o violation exists, avoid an adverse earlier ruling, and don't let the client cease all rent without a deposit strategy

issueModules.title: MDL 302-a: three-element test, the deposit amount, and the proof-of-mailing trap
issueModules.when: Building any 302-a defense. Tenant must affirmatively plead AND prove: (1) a deposit of the amount due under the petition; (2) a violation not certified corrected within six months of notice; and (3) proof DHPD mailed the NOV to the owner. DEPOSIT amount (Ackie): limited to the sum alleged in the petition (plus petitioner-moved amendments) — a tenant cannot be required to deposit more. ABATEMENT period (President St./Edwards): the Ackie deposit cap is a pre-merits rule and does NOT limit the abatement — on the merits the court can award 100% for the full period the violation stayed uncorrected, even past the petition period. PROOF-OF-MAILING trap (Edwards/170 NY Props): tenant LOST despite open violations because disorganized DHPD bulk-mailing records failed to prove mailing; subpoena DHPD directly for proof of service of the specific NOVs
issueModules.strength: App Term (Ackie) + trial-level framework (Edwards, President St., Food First)
issueModules.posture: FAVORABLE on deposit/abatement; CAUTION on proof of mailing

issueModules.title: Rent-control status persists through temporary exemption; status cannot be conferred by waiver/estoppel; FMRA four-year bar inapplicable when regulatory status itself is at issue
issueModules.when: Overcharge/regulatory-status dispute where the apartment was historically rent-controlled. A rent-controlled apartment retains that status even if temporarily exempt; coverage cannot be created by waiver or estoppel; and the four-year FMRA limit does not apply where the dispute is about regulatory status. Initial stabilized rent after rent control is set under RSC 2521.1(a)(1) (Liggett). In Nebbou both SJ motions were DENIED — fact issues on prior status
issueModules.strength: trial-level framework citing 1st Dept and Court of Appeals authority

issueModules.title: Succession: co-residency + family-member status (traditional or nontraditional)
issueModules.when: Holdover/licensee against an occupant claiming succession. Successor must show (a) a qualifying family relationship — traditional or nontraditional via Braschi emotional/financial commitment & interdependence — and (b) primary co-residency for 2 years (1 year if elderly/disabled) before the tenant's permanent vacatur. PERMANENT-VACATUR DATE: where a tenant keeps signing renewals after vacating and living elsewhere, the vacatur date is the expiration of the last renewal (Third Lenox); but that rule does NOT apply where the record tenant primarily resided and died during the renewal and a co-tenant simply didn't renew (Singh — landlord SJ denied; fact issues on the 2-year period). WINS: Sequin (disabled son, 1-yr co-residency). LOSSES to distinguish: Ordonez (nontraditional claim failed — 'one-sided', asymmetric, large age gap) and Marzullo (failed to prove the co-residency period; also barred by successive-SJ rule)
issueModules.strength: Court of Appeals standard (Braschi) applied at App Term / trial level
issueModules.posture: MIXED — favorable framework (Sequin, Singh) with landlord-favorable cautions (Ordonez, Marzullo)

issueModules.title: Sublet / short-term-hosting profiteering is CURABLE unless the tenant charged ABOVE a proportionate share
issueModules.when: Holdover alleging illegal profiteering from subtenants/short-term guests. Charging more than the tenant's proportionate share of rent for the days hosted is incurable profiteering (Goldstein v Lipetz line). But where the per-diem did NOT exceed the tenant's share for the days actually hosting, or the excess is de minimis (Jordaan: $136.25 from 21 guests over 18 months; tenant ceased and cured), it is not incurable and does not warrant forfeiture without an opportunity to cure — especially for a long-term, elderly, regulated tenant (Reynolds). RSC 2525.6(b) supplies the calculation methodology

issueModules.title: Tenant harassment counterclaim (HMC 27-2004/27-2005(d)): buyout pressure + repeated failure to correct hazardous violations
issueModules.when: Landlord's agents repeatedly pressured the tenant to vacate / made buyout offers (Guzman, Howell: landlord-initiated buyout solicitation = harassment under 27-2004(a)(48)(f-2)), or the landlord repeatedly failed to correct hazardous/immediately-hazardous violations in time (Phillip). Remedies: HPD Class 'C' violation, civil penalties (27-2115(m)(2): not less than $2,000), injunction, compensatory and possible punitive damages and fees (27-2115(o)). CAUTION (Guzman): where the lawful rent is zero from a c/o bar, there is no baseline to measure a rent-abatement-style compensatory award, so the court may award only nominal/statutory compensatory damages

issueModules.title: PRACTICE TRAP: an amended answer supersedes the original — defenses/counterclaims not carried forward are WAIVED
issueModules.when: Any time you amend an answer. The amended answer supersedes the prior pleading in all respects. In Edwards (170 NY Props) the amended answer dropped the habitability defense, so the tenant could not get a habitability abatement. Re-plead every defense and counterclaim you intend to keep. Leave to amend is freely granted absent prejudice/surprise unless palpably insufficient (Badesch; Creston)
issueModules.strength: settled appellate rule, illustrated in Edwards; liberal-amendment standard (Badesch)

issueModules.title: An amended answer in a summary proceeding may be verified by counsel (or unverified)
issueModules.when: Landlord objects that the amended answer is verified by the attorney rather than the respondent. RPAPL 743 allows a respondent to answer orally or in an unverified written answer; no authority requires amended answers to be treated differently

issueModules.title: Specific factual denial of service rebuts the affidavit and warrants a traverse hearing
issueModules.when: Challenging service. A bare/conclusory denial of receipt is insufficient (and an unsworn denial in a memo, or new facts first raised in reply, won't do). But a specific factual denial — e.g., that the papers were left near the building intercom rather than affixed to the door — raises an issue of fact requiring a traverse (Davidson). Affix-and-mail service under CPLR 308(4) requires genuine due diligence first
issueModules.posture: FAVORABLE — plead service defenses with specific sworn facts

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): retaliatory eviction must be pleaded; good-faith nonpayment basis defeats it
issueModules.when: Retaliatory-eviction defense. Unpreserved if first raised on appeal and not in the (amended) answer (Bei Yang). Does not apply where the landlord acted in good faith on a legitimate ground such as nonpayment. Constructive eviction and noise-habitability claims turn on credibility and require deprivation of essential residential functions
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — plead retaliation in the answer with specific facts; anticipate a good-faith rebuttal

issueModules.title: DISTINGUISH (landlord-favorable): a single sufficiently serious incident can constitute nuisance
issueModules.when: Nuisance holdover. No strict quantitative test; a court weighs quantitative and qualitative aspects, and one incident serious enough (in Gray, a shooting in the apartment) may survive a motion to dismiss. On a 3211 motion the court takes the notice's allegations as true
issueModules.posture: UNFAVORABLE — distinguish by isolating the incident and showing it is non-recurring, not part of a pattern (cf. T Building on proof at a hearing)

issueModules.title: HOME Program affordability period is a 30-year minimum, not a maximum (HDFC co-op resale restrictions)
issueModules.when: Niche / contextual: an HDFC cooperative seeks to escape resale/affordability restrictions by arguing the HOME Program period was only 15 years. The regulation sets a MINIMUM; the recorded Regulatory Agreement / offering plan / proprietary lease controlled a 30-year period, and DHCR/HTFC retain approval authority until expiry. Low direct relevance to summary-proceeding tenant defense

issueModules.title: Setting the legal regulated rent: ALL available rent history counts, regardless of date
issueModules.when: When the issue is calculating/determining the legal regulated rent (as opposed to awarding overcharge damages), RSL 26-516(h) requires consideration of all available rent history regardless of date. Regina's bar on retroactive overcharge-damage calculation does NOT limit rent-setting; the four-year/lookback limit is inapplicable to determining regulated status or the lawful rent

issueModules.title: Out-of-court surrender / buyout agreements are unenforceable where the bargain was coerced or the landlord lacked cause
issueModules.when: Landlord seeks to enforce an out-of-court surrender or buyout (or sues the former tenant as a licensee after one). Unenforceable where the landlord had no independent cause to evict, the landlord (not the tenant) initiated the negotiation, the tenant was misled (e.g., a false threat not to renew a rent-stabilized lease the tenant is entitled to by law), the tenant lacked counsel, and/or the tenant is vulnerable (poverty, disability, Section 8). The landlord-initiated solicitation also independently constitutes harassment (HMC 27-2004(a)(48)(f-2))

issueModules.title: A summary proceeding is purely possessory; it does not lie against a respondent who vacated before commencement
issueModules.when: A respondent (e.g., a co-lessee) who permanently vacated and ceased physical possession BEFORE the proceeding was commenced is entitled to dismissal — a summary proceeding cannot be maintained against someone not in possession at commencement. Distinguish the continuing-jurisdiction rule, which applies only where the respondent was in possession at commencement and vacated during the pendency. Any U&O claim against the vacated party is for a plenary action
issueModules.strength: trial-level (Guthrie, J.) applying settled appellate law

issueModules.title: A nonpayment proceeding does not lie without a rental agreement in effect at commencement
issueModules.when: Nonpayment where no lease/rental agreement was in effect when the proceeding was commenced (e.g., the last renewal had expired and none was offered/executed). There is no basis for a nonpayment proceeding, and the petition may be dismissed (Whaley affirmed a sua sponte dismissal on this ground)

issueModules.title: A rent-stabilized holdover requires a valid RSC 2524.2 notice of termination; court may dismiss sua sponte on the papers (CPLR 409(b))
issueModules.when: Holdover against a rent-stabilized tenant where the only predicate served was a notice to cure / default notice that did not terminate the tenancy or state a surrender date. Proof of a valid termination notice is a necessary element; absent it, the petition fails, and under CPLR 409(b) the court may make a summary determination and dismiss even sua sponte (Simpson dismissed on a ground the tenant hadn't raised)

issueModules.title: Forfeiting a tenancy on breach of a probationary nuisance stipulation requires competent, non-hearsay proof of SUBSTANTIAL interference
issueModules.when: Landlord moves to restore for a judgment/warrant alleging breach of a probationary (nuisance) stipulation. The stipulation remains under court supervision; the landlord must prove a breach at a hearing by competent evidence. Isolated incidents with no affected resident present, generalized testimony ('always drinking,' 'disrespectful'), and hearsay accounts of threats are insufficient — a judgment cannot rest on hearsay alone (T Building denied the warrant)

issueModules.title: Disqualifying opposing counsel: prior relationship + substantially related matter + materially adverse interests
issueModules.when: Niche / motion practice: moving to disqualify opposing counsel under RPC 1.9. Movant must show (1) a prior attorney-client relationship, (2) the matters are substantially related, and (3) interests are materially adverse; all three create an irrebuttable presumption. Courts balance avoiding the appearance of impropriety against the right to counsel of choice and the risk of tactical misuse. A single limited prior consultation, unrelated to the present issue, is insufficient
